lesson.text: Every AI system has three moments: before, during, and after.

Training is building the model — collecting examples, labeling them, fitting weights. Inference is using the model — a live request runs through the network right now. Output is what comes out the other side — a reply, a score, a diagnosis, a decision.

The three phases carry different obligations. Bias mitigation is a training-phase fix — you correct the data before the model ever learns from it. Explainability requirements attach to output — they travel with the decision, not the dataset.

Governance mistakes usually come from misrouting a step to the wrong phase. "Loading the trained model's weights into GPU memory" sounds like setup, but it happens every time the server starts serving requests — that makes it inference infrastructure, not training. Get the phase wrong and you audit the wrong control.

A credit-scoring model that returns "approved, score 740" alongside "top reasons: stable income, low debt, long history" is producing two outputs at once — a decision and its explanation. Both are auditable outputs; neither is training data.

quiz.q: RetailMax gathers 18 months of real customer support chats to teach their new chatbot what good answers look like. Which phase is this?
explain: Correct — training data is collected before the model is built. These conversations become the examples the AI learns from.
Inference is what happens when a live user sends the finished chatbot a new question — this step happens before the chatbot even exists.
Output is what the finished model produces for a user — this is raw material for building the model, not a result it generated.

quiz.q: A server reads the trained model file from disk and loads it into GPU memory so it can start answering requests. Which phase?
explain: It sounds like setup, but the weights were already trained — nothing is being learned here, just prepared to serve.
Correct — loading weights to serve predictions is inference infrastructure. It happens every time the server starts up, not when the model is built.
Nothing has been produced yet at this point — the model hasn't processed any input.

quiz.q: A credit-scoring system returns "Loan approved, score 740" plus "Top reasons: stable income, low debt, long history." What is the explanation list?
explain: The explanation is generated per applicant, at decision time — it isn't an example fed to the model beforehand.
It isn't about processing efficiency — it's a result the model hands back alongside the decision.
Correct — explainability features are outputs. They accompany the prediction to help a human understand why the model decided what it did.
